topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office seemed to hum louder than usual that Tuesday afternoon. I was wrestling with a particularly stubborn spreadsheet, the numbers blurring before my eyes. Then, Sarah walked past, clutching a sleek, expensive-looking laptop bag. She paused near my desk, offering a brief, polite nod. "Rough day?" she asked, her voice light. Later that week, I stumbled upon the quarterly compensation report. My breath hitched in my throat when I saw the figures. Sarah’s salary was nearly double mine. I stared at the screen, my fingers trembling slightly as I reread the data. The world seemed to tilt on its axis; the coffee in my mug suddenly tasted like ash. I spent the rest of the afternoon just staring at the wall, unable to focus on anything but the stark reality of the disparity.

[story 2]

It happened during the monthly budget review. I was presenting my department’s projections, feeling confident, until the slide detailing individual departmental earnings came up. My gaze snagged on the entry for Mark, a junior analyst who handled the data entry for the entire division. The number displayed was astronomical. I blinked hard, forcing my eyes to refocus. My jaw went slack, and I instinctively gripped the edge of the podium until my knuckles turned white. When Mark, oblivious, cheerfully explained his recent promotion, his easy confidence felt like a physical weight pressing down on me. I managed a weak, "That's... impressive," my voice sounding strangely thin. I excused myself early, needing to walk away from the room before I started shaking.

[story 3]

I found out while browsing the internal HR portal, a place I usually avoided. I clicked on the salary comparison tool, and the data materialized on the screen. My vision tunneled. I leaned closer to the monitor, my heart beginning a frantic rhythm against my ribs. The figure next to 'Junior Associate' was so far removed from mine it felt like looking at a different planet. I pulled my hand back as if burned, my skin prickling with an odd, cold sensation. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. I walked to the window, staring out at the city skyline, feeling completely detached, as if the air itself had become too thin to breathe normally.
